Model Performance

  
Evaluation results on the validation set (640 images) after 10 epochs of training on 3,845 images using Faster R-CNN with ResNet-50 FPN backbone.


  

    

      
93%

      
Avg Recall

    

    

      
84%

      
Avg Precision

    

    

      
10

      
Epochs

    

    

      
22,757

      
Training Boxes

    

  


  

    
Training Loss Curve

    

      
      
Total training loss dropped from 0.427 at epoch 1 to 0.127 at epoch 10 — a 70% reduction. The classifier and box regression losses both converge smoothly, indicating stable learning. The sharp drop at epoch 8 corresponds to the learning rate scheduler stepping down.

    

  


  

    

      
Per-Class Recall

      

        
        
All classes exceed 80% recall. Korean melon achieves perfect recall (1.00). Lemon is the weakest at 0.80 due to visual similarity with cantaloupe in the dataset.

      

    

    

      
Precision & Recall per Class

      

        
        
Recall consistently exceeds precision across all classes, indicating the model tends to over-detect. Pineapple and durian show the best balance (precision 0.93 and 0.92 respectively).

      

    

  


  

    

      
Confidence Score Distribution

      

        
        
All classes show median confidence well above the 0.5 threshold. Korean melon has the tightest distribution (0.90–1.00), while lemon and grape show wider spread, reflecting their lower precision scores.

      

    

    

      
Ground Truth vs Detected Count

      

        
        
Tangerine is the most over-detected class (193 detected vs 163 GT). Cantaloupe is the most balanced. Over-detection is a known characteristic of Faster R-CNN on dense fruit images.

      

    

  


  

    
Normalized Confusion Matrix

    

      
      
Near-perfect diagonal shows the model correctly classifies almost all fruits. The only notable off-diagonal value is lemon → dragon_fruit (0.16), which is a direct consequence of dataset label noise where some lemon-shaped fruits were mislabelled as dragon fruit in the source Kaggle dataset.

    

  


  

    
Per-Class Prediction Samples

    

      
      
Colored boxes = model predictions, gray dashed = ground truth. The model correctly identifies most fruits with high confidence. Note the korean_melon sample (center-left) shows durian images labelled as korean_melon in the original dataset — a label noise issue, not a model error.

    

  











  
Dataset Label Noise Analysis

  
During training and evaluation, systematic mislabelling was identified in the source Kaggle Fruit Object Detection dataset (eunpyohong, 2023). These findings are documented here for transparency and academic integrity.


  

    

      
⚠️ Durian → Korean Melon Confirmed

      
Multiple durian images (spiky exterior, yellow flesh) were labelled as korean_melon in the training set. Both fruits have textured exteriors, likely causing annotator confusion. The model learned this incorrect association faithfully.

    

    

      
⚠️ Cantaloupe → Durian Confirmed

      
Several cantaloupe images (oval, netted skin) were labelled as durian. This explains why the model predicts "durian: 1.00" on clear cantaloupe images — it is correctly following the (incorrect) training labels.

    

    

      
⚠️ Mixed Fruit Boxes Confirmed

      
Several gift box images containing multiple fruit types were assigned a single class label. This causes the model to misidentify non-target fruits in those images as the labelled class.

Key Finding

    

      
The model achieves near-perfect performance on correctly labelled classes (apple 99%, tangerine 99%, watermelon 100%, grape 100%), confirming the training pipeline and architecture are sound.

      
Misclassifications are concentrated in exotic Asian fruit classes where the original dataset annotators appear to have made systematic errors — likely due to unfamiliarity with durian, korean melon, and cantaloupe. These three classes share overlapping visual features (oval shape, textured skin, similar coloration) that make them difficult to distinguish without domain expertise.

      
This finding highlights a critical principle in applied machine learning: model performance is bounded by dataset quality. A well-trained model cannot overcome systematically incorrect ground truth labels. Future work should include a manual audit of the exotic fruit class annotations and potential re-labelling of the approximately 15–20% of affected images.

    

  











  
About This Service

  
A Faster R-CNN fruit detection microservice built for EAI6010 Module 5 at Northeastern University Vancouver.


  

    

      
Architecture

      

        
Model: Faster R-CNN

        
Backbone: ResNet-50 with Feature Pyramid Network (FPN)

        
Pretrained: COCO (91 classes), fine-tuned on fruit dataset

        
Classes: 11 fruit + 1 background = 12 total

        
Framework: PyTorch 2.2 (CPU inference)

        
API: FastAPI + Uvicorn

        
Hosting: HuggingFace Spaces (Docker, CPU Basic)

      

    

    

      
Training Details

      

        
Dataset: Kaggle Fruit Object Detection (eunpyohong)

        
Train images: 3,845

        
Validation images: 640

        
Bounding boxes: 22,757 total

        
Epochs: 10

        
Batch size: 4

        
Optimizer: SGD (LR=0.005, momentum=0.9)

        
LR Scheduler: StepLR (step=7, gamma=0.1)

      

    

  


  

    
API Endpoints

    

      

        
MethodEndpointDescription

        
          
GET/This web interface

          
POST/predictUpload image, returns detections with bounding boxes and confidence scores

          
GET/healthService health check — returns model status and supported classes

          
GET/docsInteractive Swagger UI for API testing
